chapter_summary: Garuda, before setting out to fetch the amrita (nectar), asks his mother Vinata what he may eat to sustain himself on the way. Vinata directs him to a great settlement of Nishadas (fish-eating forest dwellers) on the shore of the ocean and tells him to devour many thousands of them. She sternly warns him, however, that he must never form the intent to kill a Brahmana, for a Brahmana is unslayable by all beings and is like fire: an angered brahmana becomes as fire, sun, poison, and weapon; the brahmana is the foremost eater among beings, best of the varnas, a father and a guru. Garuda asks how he is to recognize a Brahmana, and Vinata answers that one who, on entering his throat like a swallowed fishhook, burns him like a live coal is to be known as a brahmana-bull. With a mother's love she blesses him: may the wind guard his wings, the moon his back, the fire his head, and the sun all of him; she herself remains ever devoted to his peace and welfare and bids him go a safe road to accomplish his purpose. Garuda then spreads his wings and flies into the sky, descending upon the Nishadas like the great Antaka (Death) at the end of time. He raises a vast cloud of dust reaching the sky, dries up the water in the belly of the ocean, and shakes the nearby mountains. Opening his enormous mouth he blocks the path of the Nishadas; bewildered by the dust and wind, they rush by thousands into his gaping maw as birds fly into the sky, and the mighty lord of birds devours the fish-eating Nishadas.

story.title: Garuda Devours the Nishadas
story.arc: Bound for the amrita, Garuda asks his mother Vinata what he may eat. She sends him to feast on the Nishadas but lays down an absolute prohibition: never harm a Brahmana, who is unslayable and like fire. Taught to recognize a Brahmana by the burning he causes in the throat, and armed with a mother's blessing over every part of his body, Garuda flies forth, descends like Death upon the Nishadas, dries the sea, opens his vast maw, and swallows them by the thousands.
story.timeline_note: Part of the Astika-parva / Garuda cycle in Adi Parva, immediately before Garuda's quest to seize the amrita.
story.comic_potential: The image of a mother packing her son a meal of 'thousands of forest-dwellers' with a strict 'but absolutely no Brahmanas, dear' rule, plus the dietary test of waiting to feel a coal burn in your throat, has rich dark-comic potential.

teachings.teaching: A Brahmana must never be harmed: he is unslayable by all beings and, when angered, becomes as fire, sun, poison, and weapon.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya framework the brahmana symbolizes the awakened, purified consciousness; its inviolability marks the inner principle that must be protected and never assaulted, since turning against it scorches the aggressor like fire.

teachings.teaching: The Brahmana is the foremost eater among beings, best of the varnas, a father and a guru.
teachings.lens_note: The brahmana as 'father and guru' points to the inner guru-principle; in Sharma's lens the highest faculty of the seeker takes precedence and is honored first, the receiver of the offering.

teachings.teaching: A Brahmana is recognized not by outward sign but by an inner effect: like a swallowed fishhook or a live coal, he burns the throat of one who would consume him.
teachings.lens_note: For Sharma the test of the genuine is experiential, not external: the true spiritual reality is known by the unmistakable inner heat it produces, the burning that warns the unworthy from devouring what is sacred.

teachings.teaching: A mother's blessing invokes the cosmic powers to guard each part of the body: wind the wings, moon the back, fire the head, sun the whole.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya view the body is protected and energized by the cosmic forces (wind/prana, lunar and solar currents, the inner fire); the mother's benediction maps the deities onto the body as the awakened energies that guard the practitioner on the path.
